Predicting Housing Prices Using Multiple Regression

Authors

Author 1

Author 2

Author 3

Author 4

Abstract

This study investigates which property characteristics most influence residential sale prices using data from the Saratoga, New York housing market. After cleaning 1,736 observations and applying stepwise variable selection, a multiple regression model with ten predictors was fit to 1,721 transactions. The final model explains approximately 65% of the variation in sale price (adjusted R² = 0.645). Living area, land value, and bathrooms emerged as the strongest positive drivers of price, while waterfront access carried the single largest premium at roughly $120,000. The model provides useful insight into housing value drivers but is limited by right-skewed residuals and the absence of neighbourhood-level covariates.

Introduction

What property characteristics most influence residential sale prices? Answering this question has practical value for home buyers evaluating fair market value, sellers setting listing prices, and appraisers performing valuations. This report builds a multiple linear regression model to predict sale price from structural, locational, and categorical property features recorded in the Saratoga housing dataset.

Data Set

The dataset records residential property sales from Saratoga, New York and is commonly used for regression instruction. The raw file contained 1,736 observations across 17 variables. The administrative Test column — a binary partition indicator — was excluded prior to analysis, leaving 16 predictors and one response variable, Price (USD).

Data cleaning. One duplicate row was removed, as exact matches across all 16 variables more plausibly reflect data duplication than distinct transactions. Six additional observations were excluded for implausible values: three where Price < Land.Value (implying negative structural value) and three with zero-valued structural variables (Lot.Size = 0 or Bathrooms = 0). The final cleaned dataset contains 1721 observations and no missing values.

Variables. Predictors span continuous measures (Lot.Size, Age, Land.Value, Living.Area, Pct.College, Bedrooms, Fireplaces, Bathrooms, Rooms), binary indicators (Waterfront, New.Construct, Central.Air), and categorical factors (Fuel.Type, Heat.Type, Sewer.Type). Four sparse Fuel.Type levels (each n ≤ 3) were collapsed into “Other.”

Summary statistics for key variables.
name Mean SD Min Max
Age 28 30 0 225
Bathrooms 2 1 1 4
Land.Value 34418 33879 200 255000
Living.Area 1756 620 616 5228
Lot.Size 1 1 0 12
Price 211887 97474 20000 775000
Rooms 7 2 2 12

Analysis

Variable selection. A full model with all 15 predictors was fit first. Stepwise selection using AIC (direction = “both”) was then applied. Both forward and backward procedures converged on the same ten-predictor model, so only the final model is reported. The dropped variables — Fuel.Type, Heat.Type, Sewer.Type, Pct.College, and Fireplaces — were individually and jointly non-significant (all p > 0.08 in the full model).

Assumptions. Each observation represents a distinct property transaction, so the independence assumption (that rows are independent of one another) is reasonable. Residual diagnostic plots are shown below.

The residuals-versus-fitted plot shows no strong curvature, supporting the linearity assumption. However, the spread of residuals fans outward at higher fitted values, indicating some heteroscedasticity, and the Q–Q plot reveals right-skew in the upper tail. These departures suggest that coefficient estimates remain unbiased but standard errors may be somewhat understated for the highest-priced homes. A log transformation of Price was explored but altered the set of significant predictors and complicated coefficient interpretation; the untransformed model was retained for clarity, and the residual non-normality is noted as a limitation.

Land.Value 0.92 0.05 0.00 0.82 1.01
New.Construct1 -44620.17 7117.58 0.00 -58580.27 -30660.07
Central.Air1 9814.09 3381.14 0.00 3182.48 16445.71
Heat.TypeHot Air 9700.90 4048.34 0.02 1760.67 17641.13
Heat.TypeHot Water -834.64 5200.37 0.87 -11034.42 9365.13
Heat.TypeNone -33595.05 24621.00 0.17 -81885.56 14695.46

Bathrooms 23964.75 3324.32 0.00 17444.58 30484.93
Rooms 3142.26 958.50 0.00 1262.31 5022.21

The final model has an adjusted R² of 0.647, a residual standard error of $57,909, and all ten retained predictors are significant at α = 0.05.

Key coefficient interpretations (holding other predictors constant):

  • Living.Area: each additional 100 sq ft of living space is associated with an approximately $6,936 increase in price (range in data: 616 – 5,228 sq ft).
  • Land.Value: a $10,000 increase in assessed land value corresponds to roughly $9,167 higher sale price.
  • Bathrooms: each additional bathroom adds about $23,965 (range: 1–4.5).
  • Waterfront: waterfront properties sell for approximately $119,175 more, though this estimate is based on only 14 waterfront homes and should be interpreted with caution.
  • New.Construct: newly constructed homes are associated with a $44,620 lower price after controlling for other features, likely because new builds in this market tend to occupy lower-valued land.
  • Bedrooms: an additional bedroom is associated with a $8,109 decrease in price once living area and rooms are held constant, reflecting the trade-off between more bedrooms and smaller room sizes within a fixed footprint.

Discussion & Conclusion

This model explains roughly 65% of the variance in Saratoga housing prices. Living area, land value, and bathrooms are the strongest positive value drivers, while waterfront access carries the single largest price premium. These findings are consistent with conventional real-estate intuition: space, location quality, and desirable amenities command higher prices.

Limitations. The data are observational, so no causal claims can be made — for instance, adding a bathroom does not guarantee a $23,000 return. The right-skewed residuals and mild heteroscedasticity mean that predictions for the most expensive homes carry wider uncertainty than the model implies. Additionally, potentially important predictors such as school district, neighbourhood desirability, and property condition are not captured. The dataset is specific to Saratoga, NY, and the model may not transfer to other housing markets with different price distributions or regional preferences.

Overcoming limitations. Future work could incorporate spatial variables (e.g. distance to amenities, school ratings) and apply robust standard errors or a log transformation to better handle residual non-normality. Expanding the dataset across multiple markets would improve generalisability.

Conclusion. A ten-predictor linear regression provides a useful, interpretable summary of the factors driving housing prices in Saratoga. While not a substitute for a professional appraisal, the model identifies living area, land value, bathrooms, and waterfront access as the most influential value determinants and can serve as a starting point for more refined pricing tools.

Appendix

Stepwise model formula:
Price ~ Lot.Size + Waterfront + Age + Land.Value + New.Construct + 
    Central.Air + Heat.Type + Living.Area + Bedrooms + Bathrooms + 
    Rooms

Call:
lm(formula = Price ~ Lot.Size + Waterfront + Age + Land.Value + 
    New.Construct + Central.Air + Heat.Type + Living.Area + Bedrooms + 
    Bathrooms + Rooms, data = train)
